mappings.rationaleAI: Mao is the central figure of section 975's A division from the development of Maoist ideology (A.4.d) through the Great Leap Forward, Cultural Revolution (A.5.c.iii), and the challenge to Soviet communism (A.5.c.iv); his death in 1976 marks a turning point in A.5.e.

China is the direct subject of the entire A division, from the early republic (1912–20) through the Kuomintang-Communist struggle, the war against Japan, PRC formation, Great Leap Forward, Cultural Revolution, Deng era reforms, and the Nationalist government in Taiwan.

South Korea is the direct subject of C.2, covering the Korean War (C.2.b), the regimes of Syngman Rhee and Park Chung-hee, democratisation, and economic development through the administrations of Kim Dae-jung and Roh Moo-hyun (C.2.c).

The Cold War frames A.5.d (China's international relations and UN representation), B.2.d.ii (Japan's post-independence international relations, radical political movements, and politics of moderation), and C.2.a–b (division of Korea and the Korean War as a Cold War proxy conflict).

The United States is explicitly cited as a central actor: providing military aid to China (A.3.b), rapprochement with the PRC (A.5.d), occupying and democratising Japan 1945–52 (B.2.d.i), economic tensions with Japan (B.2.d.iii), and military intervention in the Korean War (C.2.b).

WWII is directly covered across multiple nodes: Communist-Nationalist cooperation and the war against Japan (A.3), Japan's Axis alignment and Pacific offensives (B.2.b), Japan's surrender (B.2.c), and the end of Japanese colonial rule in Korea (C.1).
Hong Kong is explicitly named in A.5.d (the 1984 Hong Kong agreement with Great Britain) and A.5.e (restoration of Chinese rule in Hong Kong in 1997 under Deng Xiaoping's successors).

Japan is the direct subject of the entire B division, covering early-20th-century constitutional government, the rise of militarism and imperial expansion, WWII defeat and US occupation, postwar democratisation, and the economic miracle and long LDP dominance.
East Asia as a regional article encompasses all three divisions of section 975 — China (A), Japan (B), and Korea (C) — providing geographic, cultural, and historical context for the entire section.

WWI is explicitly the subject of A.1.a (Chinese involvement in WWI and early power struggles) and A.1.i (Japanese territorial gains during the war and Yuan Shih-k'ai's ambitions), with A.1.i.ii covering China's conflict over war entry and the formation of a rival southern government.

Beijing (Peking) is directly named in A.5.c.iii (resistance to Peking during the Cultural Revolution) and serves as the political and administrative capital across the PRC period covered by A.5, including the 1989 pro-democracy repression described in A.5.e.

Section A.4 covers the development of Kuomintang and Chinese Communist ideologies; A.4.d specifically covers Maoist ideology as a Chinese adaptation of Marxism-Leninism, making Marx's foundational theory directly relevant to understanding the ideological debates outlined.

Stalin's USSR is the reference point for A.5.c.iv (the Chinese challenge to Soviet communism in the Maoist era) and for C.2.a (Soviet military aid to North Korea and the US-Soviet dispute over the two Korean republics after 1945).

Russia/USSR figures in A.5.c.iv (friction between China and the Soviet Union, the Sino-Soviet split), A.5.d (continued friction with the USSR in China's international relations), and C.2.a (Soviet military aid and the division of Korea).

Imperialism is central to A.1 (the continued presence of foreign interests in China, Yuan's monarchist ambitions amid Japanese pressure) and A.2.b (foreign interests and warlord-era China), as well as Japan's own imperialist expansion described in B.2.a–b (Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere, Axis alignment).

Japan's expansion into French Indochina (including Vietnam) is part of the Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere proclaimed in B.2.b and Japan's Southeast Asian offensives in B.2.c, giving Vietnam relevance as a theatre of Japanese imperial expansion.

The Philippines is one of the key territories captured in Japan's early Southeast Asian offensives described in B.2.c, and its American administration meant the Philippines was a primary US-Japan theatre, directly relevant to Japan's initial successes and eventual surrender.

Colonial presence by Western powers and Japan provides the structural backdrop for Chinese nationalist resistance (A.1, A.2.b — foreign interests in China, treaty ports) and directly describes the content of C.1 (Japanese colonial military rule in Korea 1910–45).
